subject: general-histology
title: General Histology — Light Microscope Slide ID
description: Flashcards: study each light-microscopy lab specimen, then hit Answer to reveal the features to identify. Slides from the General Histology review slideshow; features from the GH specimen notes.

questions.imageAlt: Transverse section of a tubular organ: a folded, star-shaped lumen lined by several layers of rounded epithelial cells, wrapped by pink smooth muscle.
questions.imageCaption: Light micrograph, H&E — low & high power

explanation.text:
Ureter — identified by the urothelium.
• Mucosa lined by transitional epithelium (urothelium): rounded, dome-shaped 'umbrella' surface cells; a folded, star-shaped lumen
• Lamina propria — no glands
• Muscularis: smooth muscle — spindle cells, one oval central nucleus, no cross-striations, indistinct cell borders
Not esophagus (stratified squamous) or trachea (respiratory epithelium + cartilage).
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #1 · slideshow p.3

questions.imageAlt: Cross-sections of blood vessels; one with a thick muscular wall and round lumen, plus an elastic-stained panel showing wavy black laminae within the wall.
questions.imageCaption: Light micrographs — H&E (left) and elastic / Weigert stain (right)

explanation.text:
Artery vs vein.
ARTERY: thick muscular wall (tunica media); round lumen; internal & external elastic laminae (best seen with elastic stain); thin adventitia; no valves.
VEIN: thin media; wider, slit-like lumen; less elastin; abundant adventitia; valves (paired intimal folds).
The round lumen + thick media + elastic laminae give it away as the artery.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #11 · slideshow p.4
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated light micrographs (H&E and elastic stain) comparing artery and vein.

questions.imageAlt: Surface tissue with a layered squamous epithelium over connective tissue containing hair follicles, small glands and fat.

explanation.text:
Skin (thin / hairy skin) — the appendages are the clue.
• Epidermis: stratified squamous keratinized epithelium
• Dermis: papillary layer (loose, vascular CT) over reticular layer (dense irregular CT)
• Appendages: hair follicles with sebaceous glands (simple alveolar) and sweat glands (simple coiled tubular)
• Hypodermis: adipose tissue (subcutaneous fat), lobulated by CT septa
Other stratified-squamous organs (esophagus, vagina, cornea) lack hair follicles & skin glands.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #2 · slideshow p.6

questions.imageAlt: Mucosa with a flat surface and densely packed straight tubular glands lined by columnar cells with many goblet cells; smooth muscle beneath.

explanation.text:
Large intestine (colon) — no villi, abundant goblet cells.
• Mucosa: simple columnar epithelium with many goblet cells; flat luminal surface (no villi)
• Lamina propria: straight, simple tubular glands (crypts of Lieberkühn)
• Muscularis: smooth muscle (spindle cells, one oval central nucleus, no striations)
Small intestine would show villi; stomach shows gastric pits.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #3 · slideshow p.7
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated light micrograph of large intestine labelling mucosa, submucosa and muscularis propria.

questions.imageAlt: Mucosa with a stratified squamous epithelium dipping into crypts, over numerous lymphoid nodules with pale germinal centers.

explanation.text:
Palatine tonsil — surface epithelium + crypts is the key.
• Stratified squamous non-keratinized epithelium lining the surface and the crypts, densely infiltrated by lymphocytes
• Many secondary lymphoid nodules (with germinal centers) around the crypts
• Underlying dense connective tissue forms a partial capsule
Unlike a lymph node, a tonsil has surface epithelium/crypts and no subcapsular sinus.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #21 · slideshow p.8
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated light micrograph of palatine tonsil: epithelium, crypts, secondary lymphoid nodules and hemicapsule.

questions.imageAlt: Branching muscle fibers with single central nuclei and faint cross-striations; a paler group of large clear cells lies beneath the lining.

explanation.text:
Heart — cardiac muscle.
• Branching cells joined by intercalated discs; ONE central nucleus per cell; cross-striations (seen longitudinally, not in transverse)
• Pale, swollen Purkinje fibres in the sub-endocardium (few myofibrils, much glycogen, no T-tubules) — the conduction system
• Wall layers: endocardium / myocardium / epicardium
Skeletal muscle has peripheral, multiple nuclei and no branching; smooth muscle has no striations.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #14 · slideshow p.10
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated light micrograph of cardiac muscle labelling nuclei (N) and intercalated discs (I).

questions.imageAlt: A rounded CNS section with central butterfly-shaped gray matter around a small central canal, surrounded by paler white matter.

explanation.text:
Spinal cord (CNS).
• Central, butterfly-shaped gray matter (neuronal cell bodies with Nissl bodies; large motor neurons in the ventral horns) around a central canal lined by ependyma
• Surrounding white matter: myelinated axons (myelin from oligodendrocytes), with fibrous astrocytes & microglia
• Cerebellum/cortex have gray matter on the OUTSIDE; a peripheral nerve has no central canal.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #17 · slideshow p.11

questions.imageAlt: An organ divided into lobules, each with a dark outer zone and a paler central zone; no lymphoid nodules.

explanation.text:
Thymus — lobules with cortex/medulla and Hassall corpuscles, no nodules.
• Capsule sends septa that divide it into incompletely separated lobules
• Cortex: darker, packed immature T-lymphoblasts (thymocytes); fewer epithelial cells
• Medulla: paler; fewer, larger, more mature lymphocytes; more thymic epithelial cells; Hassall (thymic) corpuscles (whorls of epithelial cells)
• No lymphoid nodules and no subcapsular sinus (vs lymph node).
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #22 · slideshow p.13
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated light micrograph of thymus lobules with a Hassall's corpuscle inset.

questions.imageAlt: A bean-shaped organ with a connective-tissue capsule, a peripheral sinus, and cortical nodules over a paler medulla.

explanation.text:
Lymph node.
• Dense CT capsule with trabeculae; subcapsular (marginal) sinus just beneath
• Cortex: lymphoid nodules (B cells; with/without germinal centers)
• Paracortex: T cells + high endothelial venules
• Medulla: medullary cords (B cells, plasma cells) + medullary sinuses
The capsule + subcapsular sinus + cortex/medulla pattern (and no central arterioles) separate it from spleen and thymus.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #23 · slideshow p.14
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated light micrograph of a lymph node: capsule, cortex, paracortex and medulla.

questions.imageAlt: Lymphoid tissue forming a sheath and a nodule around a small central artery, set in a diffuse cell-rich background.

explanation.text:
Spleen — the central arteriole in the white pulp is the clue.
• Capsule + trabeculae of dense CT; reticular stroma
• White pulp (immune): periarteriolar lymphoid sheaths (T cells) around central arterioles + lymphoid nodules (B cells)
• Red pulp (blood filtration): splenic (Billroth) cords + blood-filled venous sinusoids
No cortex/medulla and no subcapsular sinus (vs lymph node); the central arteriole within lymphoid tissue is diagnostic.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #24 · slideshow p.15
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated light micrograph of spleen labelling capsule, trabecula and white/red pulp.

questions.imageAlt: Rounded cells sitting singly and in small nests within lacunae of a homogeneous matrix, bounded by a fibrous layer; no vessels.

explanation.text:
Hyaline cartilage.
• Chondrocytes sit in lacunae; isogenous (cell-nest) groups lie deeper in the matrix
• Peripheral chondroblasts beneath a perichondrium (dense irregular CT)
• Matrix rich in collagen II & GAGs; basophilic territorial matrix rings each lacuna
• Avascular — no Haversian systems, which distinguishes it from bone.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #8 · slideshow p.22
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated light micrograph of hyaline cartilage: perichondrium, chondroblasts, isogenous groups and matrix.

questions.imageAlt: Hard tissue with concentric rings of matrix arranged around central vascular canals, with small lacunae between the rings.

explanation.text:
Compact (lamellar) bone — ground preparation.
• Osteons (Haversian systems): concentric lamellae around a central Haversian canal (vessels, nerves)
• Osteocytes in lacunae, interconnected by canaliculi; interstitial & circumferential lamellae between osteons
• Volkmann (perforating) canals link Haversian canals; cement lines outline each osteon
• Cells: osteoblasts (build matrix), osteoclasts (resorb, in Howship lacunae).
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #9 · slideshow p.23
explanation.imageAlt: Annotated light micrograph of lamellar bone: osteon, osteocyte and calcified matrix.

questions.imageAlt: Long, parallel, unbranched fibers with cross-striations and flattened nuclei at the fiber periphery; a transverse section alongside.

explanation.text:
Skeletal muscle.
• Long, cylindrical, unbranched fibres; MULTIPLE nuclei at the PERIPHERY (just under the sarcolemma)
• Cross-striations of the sarcoplasm (visible longitudinally; not in transverse section)
• Connective tissue sheaths: endomysium (around fibres), perimysium (around fascicles), epimysium (around the whole muscle)
Cardiac muscle has central nuclei + branching + intercalated discs; smooth muscle is spindle-shaped with no striations.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #13 · slideshow p.24

questions.imageAlt: A smear: countless pale biconcave red cells with a few larger purple-nucleated white cells and tiny platelets.

explanation.text:
Blood smear (Giemsa).
• Mostly erythrocytes (RBCs): biconcave, anucleate, central pallor, packed with hemoglobin
• Leukocytes (WBCs): granulocytes (neutrophil, eosinophil, basophil) + agranulocytes (lymphocyte, monocyte) — recall N-L-M-E-B
• Platelets: small, anucleate cytoplasmic fragments
• Plasma is the (unstained) background matrix
A bone-marrow smear would be dominated by developing/precursor cells, not mature RBCs.
explanation.source: GH Notes on specimens #10 · slideshow p.26
